quiz_questions.question_text: How long does it take for the Moon to complete one cycle of phases?
quiz_questions.explanation: The synodic period (full cycle of lunar phases) is approximately 29.5 days.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a new moon, the Moon is located:

options.text: On the opposite side of Earth from the Sun
At a right angle to the Earth-Sun line

quiz_questions.explanation: At new moon, the Moon is roughly between Earth and the Sun, so its illuminated side faces away from us.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a waxing crescent?

options.text: A thin sliver of illumination growing after new moon
A Moon more than half illuminated and growing
A thin sliver before new moon

quiz_questions.explanation: Waxing crescent is the phase just after new moon when a small, growing sliver of the Moon is visible.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a full moon, how much of the Moon's surface facing Earth is illuminated?
quiz_questions.explanation: At full moon, the entire face of the Moon visible from Earth is illuminated by the Sun.

quiz_questions.question_text: The first quarter moon appears how illuminated?

options.text: Half illuminated (right half in Northern Hemisphere)

quiz_questions.explanation: Despite its name, the first quarter moon shows half of the Moon's visible face illuminated — it's one-quarter through the cycle.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'waning' mean in lunar phases?

options.text: The Moon is closer to Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: Waning means the lit portion of the Moon is shrinking as it moves from full moon toward new moon.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Moon shines because it:
quiz_questions.explanation: The Moon has no light of its own; it shines by reflecting sunlight.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which phase comes directly after the full moon?
quiz_questions.explanation: After the full moon, the illuminated portion begins to decrease, entering the waning gibbous phase.

quiz_questions.question_text: A gibbous moon is:

options.text: More than half but not fully illuminated

quiz_questions.explanation: Gibbous means the Moon is more than half illuminated but not yet (or no longer) full.

quiz_questions.question_text: The sidereal month (Moon's orbit relative to stars) is about:
quiz_questions.explanation: The sidereal month is about 27.3 days, shorter than the synodic month because Earth moves along its orbit.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'synodic month'?

options.text: The time between two identical lunar phases (about 29.5 days)
The time for the Moon to orbit once relative to the stars

quiz_questions.explanation: A synodic month measures the interval between the same phases (e.g., new moon to new moon), about 29.5 days.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'waxing' mean?

options.text: Growing — the illuminated portion of the Moon is increasing
Shrinking — the illuminated portion is decreasing
The Moon is moving away from Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: Waxing means the Moon's visible illuminated area is growing, from new moon through first quarter to full.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'gibbous' phase?

options.text: A phase where more than half but less than all of the Moon is illuminated
When the Moon is less than half lit

quiz_questions.explanation: Gibbous (from Latin for 'hump') describes the Moon when more than half its face is illuminated.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'earthshine'?

options.text: Sunlight reflected off Earth that faintly illuminates the dark side of the Moon
Light from the Moon reflected off Earth
The glow of lava on the Moon

quiz_questions.explanation: Earthshine is the faint glow on the Moon's unlit side, caused by sunlight bouncing off Earth and then reflecting back.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'lunar cycle'?

options.text: The complete sequence of phases from new moon to new moon
One rotation of the Moon on its axis
The Moon's journey through one zodiac sign

quiz_questions.explanation: The lunar cycle (or lunation) is the full sequence of phases, taking about 29.5 days.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'crescent' mean?

options.text: A phase where less than half of the Moon's face is illuminated
A phase where more than half is illuminated

quiz_questions.explanation: A crescent phase shows a thin, curved sliver of illumination — less than half the Moon's visible face.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'libration'?

options.text: A slight wobble in the Moon's orientation allowing us to see slightly more than 50% of its surface over time
The Moon moving faster at perigee

quiz_questions.explanation: Libration is the apparent rocking of the Moon that lets us see about 59% of its surface over time.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'sidereal month'?

options.text: The time for the Moon to complete one orbit relative to the stars (~27.3 days)
A month in the stellar calendar

quiz_questions.explanation: A sidereal month measures the Moon's orbit against the background stars, about 27.3 days.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'terminator' on the Moon?

options.text: The boundary line between the illuminated and dark portions of the Moon
A crater on the far side
The point where the Moon blocks the Sun
The edge of the Moon's atmosphere

quiz_questions.explanation: The lunar terminator is the dividing line between the sunlit and shadowed parts of the Moon's surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'tidal locking'?

options.text: When a body's rotation period equals its orbital period, keeping one face toward its partner
When two moons orbit at the same speed
When gravity pulls the Moon apart

quiz_questions.explanation: Tidal locking means the Moon rotates once per orbit, so the same face always points toward Earth.

quiz_questions.question_text: You see a thin crescent Moon just after sunset in the western sky. What phase is this?
quiz_questions.explanation: A thin crescent visible just after sunset in the west is a waxing crescent — it's growing and follows the Sun across the sky.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices that the Moon rises about 50 minutes later each day. Why?

options.text: The Moon moves eastward in its orbit, so Earth must rotate a bit extra each day to face it again
The Sun's gravity pulls the Moon back
The Moon speeds up each day

quiz_questions.explanation: The Moon advances ~13° eastward in its orbit each day, requiring about 50 extra minutes of Earth rotation to bring it back into view.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you could stand on the Moon and look at Earth, what would you observe when people on Earth see a new moon?
quiz_questions.explanation: When the Moon is between Earth and the Sun (new moon), an observer on the Moon would see Earth fully lit — the phases are opposite.

quiz_questions.question_text: A photographer wants to capture earthshine. When is the best time?

options.text: During a crescent moon phase, a few days before or after new moon
At midnight when the Moon is highest

quiz_questions.explanation: Earthshine is most visible during crescent phases when the dark part of the Moon can be faintly seen lit by light reflected from Earth.

quiz_questions.question_text: The same side of the Moon always faces Earth. A student says this means the Moon doesn't rotate. Is this correct?

options.text: No — the Moon rotates once per orbit (tidal locking), which is why the same face always points at Earth
Yes — the Moon does not rotate
No — the Moon rotates twice per orbit

quiz_questions.explanation: The Moon does rotate — its rotation period equals its orbital period (tidal locking), so one face always faces Earth.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ancient cultures used lunar calendars. What difficulty arises from using a purely lunar calendar?

options.text: 12 lunar months total about 354 days, drifting about 11 days from the solar year each year
The Moon changes color each month
Lunar calendars have too many days
The Moon isn't visible every month

quiz_questions.explanation: A 12-month lunar calendar has ~354 days, about 11 days short of a solar year, causing seasons to drift over time.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a third quarter moon, when does the Moon typically rise?
quiz_questions.explanation: The third quarter moon rises around midnight and is visible in the morning sky.

quiz_questions.question_text: A waxing gibbous Moon is visible in the sky. About how many days until the full moon?
quiz_questions.explanation: Waxing gibbous occurs between first quarter and full moon — roughly 3-4 days before full.

quiz_questions.question_text: If the Moon didn't orbit Earth but stayed fixed relative to the stars, would we still see phases?

options.text: No — without the changing Sun-Moon-Earth angle, the Moon's apparent illumination wouldn't change
Yes — the Sun would still light different parts
Yes — Earth's rotation would create phases
No — the Moon would become invisible

quiz_questions.explanation: Lunar phases result from the changing angle between the Sun, Moon, and Earth as the Moon orbits. Without orbiting, the angle wouldn't change.

quiz_questions.question_text: An observer sees the Moon at first quarter high in the sky at sunset. Where in the sky will it be at midnight?
quiz_questions.explanation: The first quarter moon is roughly 90° east of the Sun — it's highest at sunset and sets around midnight.
